scenario: i'm doing okay i just been really frustrated because with my asthma and it just seems like i can never catch a break never get better i'm al
patient_utterances: [Clinician asked: "hey betty how are you doing"] i'm doing okay i just been really frustrated because with my asthma and it just seems like i can never catch a break never get better i'm always it seems like i'm always coughing and wheezing

[Clinician asked: "i'm sorry to hear that what normally triggers your asthma type symptoms"] i find that it's really really when i get sick if i get sick with a cold i almost always know that my asthma is going to flare i did notice one time though that when i went to my aunt's house i i had a flare afterwards and i think it's because of her cat so i try to avoid going there and i have n't really had an issue since

[Clinician asked: "okay so now you mentioned that that happens somewhat a lot how frequently does this occur that you have these breathing type problems"] it seems like it happens really every month or every other month for the past six months i usually end up going to the urgent care a lot and get or my primary care doctor and so sometimes they'll give me antibiotics sometimes i'll get steroids one time it was so bad i actually ended up going to the emergency department

[Clinician asked: "wow that that can be a little bit scary so you mentioned that your primary care provider or the urgent care provider gave you medications what medications are you using for your asthma right now"] i have two inhalers one but i really just use one of them it's a blue one that seems like the only inhaler that helps me the other one when i when i try to use it it does n't work
[Clinician asked: "okay so do you think that's the albuterol inhaler is it do you remember the name like ventolin or"] yes yes that's the name of it

[Clinician asked: "is that it okay alright and then how often do you feel like you need to use that"] i would say like almost everyday i feel like i'm using my inhaler because i'm always coughing and wheezing and a couple of times a night i'll wake up as a couple times a week i'll end up waking up needing to use my inhaler as well
[Clinician asked: "okay and then when you use it at that time does it help"] it does
[Clinician asked: "okay and how many walk me through how you use that you just wake up and you take one puff or is it two puffs"] yeah i take i usually take two puffs
[Clinician asked: "okay but then you get enough relief that you can go back to sleep"] yeah for the most part

[Clinician asked: "okay now have you ever needed to be hospitalized due to your asthma symptoms you mentioned you've had to go to the er"] yeah for only once i can think of when i was young i think like around eight years old i had to be hospitalized but nothing since then

[Clinician asked: "okay i do wan na take a little bit of a a deeper dive here into your er and urgent care visits can you tell me a little bit more about those"] yeah i feel like i've had several i do n't remember the exact number but i've had several this past year they almost always have to start me on prednisone
[Clinician asked: "okay like a prednisone taper pack that type of thing"] yeah yeah sometimes they'll just give me like like a like a large dose over five days
[Clinician asked: "okay"] and then sometimes they make me take less and less and less so the over like nine or ten days
[Clinician asked: "okay so both the dosepak and then also the high dose as well okay"] yeah
[Clinician asked: "and then when was your last exacerbation"] probably about a month ago

[Clinician asked: "okay so just out of curiosity you mentioned that you you have trouble when you go over to your aunt's house because of your cat now i see here that you really like to go outside and walk and do those type of things do you have any other type of issues whenever you you go outside any any seasonal all"] no not really

[Clinician asked: "okay so you do n't have any history of seasonal allergies which is good so it looks like here that you put down that you enjoyed hiking so you do n't have any trouble getting out and going hiking and and have you tried the new trails there behind behind the wreck center"] yeah i'm really excited to i mean i have to be careful because i you know with my coughing my wheezing but i'm really i'm looking forward to getting better so i can really start hiking again

[Clinician asked: "okay now i really need you to be truthful when you answer these next questions this is important for taking a good history do you smoke any type of cigarette or tobacco product and have you ever smoked or do you smoke marijuana on a regular basis"] no i've never smoked and i do n't vape either or yeah i do n't smoke cigarettes or marijuana
[Clinician asked: "okay and then you said you do n't vape so no vaping or e-cigarette use at all"] hmmm hmmm

[Clinician asked: "okay and then are you routinely in an area where there is a lot of airborne particular like smoking in bars or around any type of of you know like race tracks or anything like that"] sometimes i go to the bars with some friends and yeah the smoking does irritate me
[Clinician asked: "okay"] so i do n't try to go there often
[Clinician asked: "okay thank you for sharing that with me so if it's okay with you i'd like to go ahead and do a quick physical exam"] okay

[Clinician asked: "now i reviewed your vitals for today and your your blood pressure is good it's one twenty eight over eighty two your respiratory rate is sixteen and your oxygen saturation is ninety nine percent on room air which is a good thing i'm gon na take a listen to your heart here your heart is regular rate "] alright thank you

[Clinician asked: "so let me tell you a little bit about my assessment and plan for your first problem of asthma your symptoms are consistent with moderate intermittent asthma i'm gon na start you on flovent one hundred ten micrograms one puff twice per day that's gon na be important for you to be consistent with your"] it does thank you
[Clinician asked: "you're welcome i'll see you in a month"] alright thanks
gold_note:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Asthma.

MEDICAL HISTORY

Patient reports history of asthma.

SOCIAL HISTORY

The patient denies the use of any type of cigarette or tobacco product, as well as marijuana. Occasionally, she will be at a bar that allows smoking inside, which does bother the patient. She enjoys being outside and hiking.

ALLERGIES

Denies seasonal allergies.

MEDICATIONS

Patient reports using an albuterol inhaler, 2 puffs.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Respiratory: Reports coughing and wheezing.

VITALS

Blood pressure: 128/82 mm Hg.
Respiratory rate: 16
O2 saturation: 99% on room air.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Neck
- General Examination: Neck is supple without lymphadenopathy. Trachea is midline. No carotid bruit.

Respiratory
- Auscultation of Lungs: Diminished lung sounds throughout with the occasional slight expiratory wheeze, bilaterally.
Cardiovascular
- Auscultation of Heart: Regular rate and rhythm. No ectopic beats. No rubs, murmurs, or gallops.

Musculoskeletal
- Examination: Strong bilateral radial pulses. No clubbing. Brisk capillary refill.

Hematology/Lymphatic/Immunology
- Palpation: No enlarged lymph nodes.

RESULTS

Pulmonary function test obtained today is reviewed and findings are consistent with asthma.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Moderate intermittent asthma.
- Medical Reasoning: The patient's symptoms and results of her PFT are consistent with moderate intermittent asthma.
- Patient Education and Counseling: I explained the importance of consistency with her daily inhaler as this will help prevent daily symptoms and the need to use the albuterol inhaler as frequently. My hope is that she has less acute exacerbations as she continues to use her inhaled steroid.
- Medical Treatment: Prescribed provided for Flovent 110 mcg 1 puff twice per day. She can continue using her Ventolin inhaler 2 puffs every 4 to 6 hours as needed. I have recommended using her albuterol inhaler 1 to 2 puffs and then monitor her breathing prior to taking an additional 1 to 2 puffs. I am also going to put together an asthma action plan for her so we know what to do based on her symptoms.

Patient Agreements: The patient understands and agrees with the recommended medical treatment plan.

INSTRUCTIONS

The patient will follow up in 1 month for reevaluation or sooner for worsening symptoms.

naive.response_text:
I understand how frustrating it must be to deal with asthma symptoms that seem to persist without end. It’s important to acknowledge how challenging this must be for you, especially when it interferes with your daily life and activities you enjoy, like hiking.

Based on what you’ve shared, it sounds like your asthma is currently classified as moderate intermittent. To help manage your symptoms more effectively, I’ve prescribed Flovent 110 mcg, which you’ll take one puff twice daily. This medication is a controller inhaler that helps reduce inflammation in your airways, which should help decrease your coughing and wheezing over time.

It’s crucial to use your albuterol inhaler as needed, but also to be consistent with the Flovent to keep your asthma under better control. We’ll monitor your progress at your follow-up appointment in a month. In the meantime, try to avoid known triggers like smoke and your aunt’s cat as much as possible. If you experience any severe symptoms or if your current treatment doesn’t seem to help, don’t hesitate to reach out or seek care promptly.

I’m here to support you through this, and let’s work together to get your asthma under better control so you can get back to enjoying all the things you love, like hiking. You’re doing the right thing by taking charge of your health, and I’m confident we can make a positive difference.

strong_prompt.response_text:
Thank you for sharing all of that with me, Betty. I can understand how frustrating it must be to feel like your asthma is never improving and that you're constantly dealing with coughing and wheezing. It’s completely reasonable to feel overwhelmed when this happens so frequently, especially when it interferes with your daily life and activities you enjoy, like hiking.

Based on what you’ve described, it sounds like your asthma is currently in a moderate, intermittent phase. To help manage this better, I’d like to start you on a maintenance inhaler called Flovent 110 mcg, which you would use one puff twice a day. This type of inhaler helps reduce inflammation in your airways over time, which can prevent asthma flare-ups [PMID ?]. It’s important to use it consistently, even when you’re feeling well, to keep your asthma under control.

I also want to make sure you’re using your albuterol inhaler correctly. You mentioned using it almost every day, sometimes twice a night, which suggests it’s helping you manage your symptoms. However, if you’re using it more than a few times a week, we might want to reassess your asthma management plan to see if adding the Flovent will help reduce your reliance on the rescue inhaler [PMID ?].

Let’s plan to check in with you in four weeks to see how you’re feeling on the new inhaler and to review your asthma management plan. Before you leave, could you tell me in your own words what we’ve agreed on today? That way, I can make sure I’ve explained everything clearly.

full.response_text:
I can hear how frustrating this has been for you — you mentioned, "I'm doing okay, I just been really frustrated because with my asthma and it just seems like I can never catch a break, never get better. I'm always it seems like I'm always coughing and wheezing." I understand how that must feel overwhelming.

Based on your symptoms and history, it's important to explore different approaches to manage your asthma. Research suggests that combining medications with non-medical strategies, like using a handheld fan and learning breathing techniques, can help reduce symptoms.

To help you get back to hiking, we should focus on managing your asthma symptoms more effectively. Studies show that setting clear goals and working together can make a big difference in achieving your health goals.

We'll review your current medications and see if adjusting them could help reduce your symptoms.

I'll introduce you to some breathing exercises and techniques that can provide immediate relief during an asthma attack.

Let's schedule a follow-up appointment in two weeks to reassess your symptoms and make any necessary adjustments to your treatment plan.

Can you tell me, in your own words, what we discussed today and what steps we'll be taking to help you manage your asthma better?

Let's plan to meet again in two weeks. In the meantime, if your symptoms get worse or if you experience any severe breathing difficulties, please call us right away.
